title: SelectionPredicate.Empty does not account for ShardSelector
author: jpbetz
body: While working on https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/140200 I noticed that `SelectionPredicate.Empty()` needs to be updated for `ShardSelector`.  I believe that without this fix, `remainingItemCount` is computed from the unfiltered window.  We should also add a test for `remainingItemCount`.

title: `exec` based readiness probes are ignored for terminating pods in v1.35
author: ryan-vance-cresta
body:
### What happened?

Readiness probes that use `exec` are skipped during pod termination for Kubernetes version `1.35`.

With `v1.35`, the readiness probe errors out with a context cancelled error. This leaves the `Ready` status as `True` until the pod is removed.

### What did you expect to happen?

Normally, when a pod is terminating the exec command still runs and updates the `Ready` status as appropriate.

This behavior is observed on other recent Kubernetes versions:
- `1.33`
- `1.34`
- `1.36`

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Please follow the readme in this public repository https://github.com/ryan-vance-cresta/k8s-readiness-probe-regression. It has a bare minimum example deployment, with `kind` configurations for different Kubernetes versions I tested against.

The readme gives all necessary commands to reproduce, but at a high level:
- Apply the example deployment, wait until running
- Terminate a pod either directly or through rollout restart
- Watch the Ready status turn false (good) or stay true (bad) 

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

Kubernetes `v1.35` is the only one observed to have this issue. Versions `1.33`, `1.34`, and `1.36` all work as expected.


### Cloud provider

This issue has been observed on AWS EKS `v1.35.5-eks-a3a0722`. It was further tested on previous versions with `kind`.

### OS version

`Amazon Linux 2023.11.20260505`


### Install tools

`kubectl`, `kind`


### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

`containerd 2.2.3`

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

_No response_

title: Evaluate enforcement of `+listType=map` and `+listMapKey=uid` for OwnerReference in ObjectMeta
author: lalitc375
body:
### What happened?
During the review of PR #140381 (Configure DV on objectMeta OwnerReference), it was observed that `OwnerReference` in `ObjectMeta` has the following tags in `staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1/types.go`:
```go
// +patchStrategy=merge
// +listType=map
// +listMapKey=uid
```
However, there are currently no corresponding handwritten validations that fully enforce these constraints. 

### Why was this not addressed in PR #140381?
As discussed in [this comment thread](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/140381#discussion_r3572186534), adding Declarative Validation (DV) for these markers right now could potentially break existing workflows. The scope of PR #140381 was strictly limited to mirroring the existing handwritten validations to ensure exact structural error reporting and deduplication parity.

### Action Items
- [ ] Investigate the impact of fully enforcing `+listMapKey=uid` and `+listType=map` on `OwnerReference`.
- [ ] Determine a safe rollout strategy if we decide to enforce these validations.
- [ ] Alternatively, if they should remain unenforced, add appropriate comments/documentation explaining this limitation.

/sig api-machinery
/kind cleanup

title: The generated test scenarios for RWX volume types dont make sense
author: gnufied
body:
### What happened?

When RWX capability is enabled we are generating CSI test with following names:

```
External Storage [Driver: csi.vsphere.vmware.com] [Testpattern: Dynamic PV (ext4)] multiVolume [Slow] should concurrently access the single volume from pods on different node
```

But this does not make sense. A RWX volume can't support ext4.  This should be fixed.

For example:

RWX volume tests shouldn't use ext4 or XFS filesystem types in their names (or in implementation).

title: Track assumed pods in a cycle to simplify gang scheduling feasibility check
author: vshkrabkov
body:
## Context
In the Kubernetes scheduler, when scheduling a pod group (gang), we need to check if the gang's `MinCount` constraint can be met. This check is performed in the `PlacementFeasible` function of the `gangscheduling` plugin.

During a pod group scheduling cycle, we evaluate pods one by one. If a pod is successfully scheduled in the simulation, it is "assumed" in the snapshot of the cache.

To determine if a subsequent pod in the same group can be scheduled, we need to know how many pods were *already scheduled* before the current cycle started (`alreadyScheduledCount`), and how many have been scheduled *during* the current cycle.

Currently, `alreadyScheduledCount` is calculated by comparing all scheduled pods in the snapshot with the list of pods that were unscheduled at the start of the cycle:

```go
unscheduledUIDs := sets.New[types.UID]()
for _, pod := range podGroupInfo.GetUnscheduledPods() {
    unscheduledUIDs.Insert(pod.UID)
}

alreadyScheduledCount := 0
for _, scheduledPod := range podGroupState.ScheduledPods() {
    if !unscheduledUIDs.Has(scheduledPod.UID) {
        alreadyScheduledCount++
    }
}
```

This approach is inefficient as it requires:
1. Building a set of UIDs for all unscheduled pods.
2. Iterating over all scheduled pods (which includes both previously scheduled and assumed in this cycle pods) and performing a lookup in the set.

## Proposal
We can simplify this logic by introducing a way to query how many pods have been assumed *during the current scheduling cycle* directly from the pod group state snapshot.

We propose adding a new method to the `PodGroupState` interface:
```go
// AssumedInThisCycleCount returns the number of assumed pods in the current scheduling cycle.
// For live state, this always returns 0.
AssumedInThisCycleCount() int
```

With this method, `alreadyScheduledCount` can be calculated as:
```go
scheduled := podGroupState.ScheduledPodsCount()
assumedInThisCycle := podGroupState.AssumedInThisCycleCount()
alreadyScheduledCount := scheduled - assumedInThisCycle
```

This eliminates the need to build the `unscheduledUIDs` set and iterate over `ScheduledPods()`.

## Proposed Changes

### 1. Framework Lister Interface
Modify [staging/src/k8s.io/kube-scheduler/framework/listers.go](file:///usr/local/google/home/vshkrabkov/Development/kubernetes/staging/src/k8s.io/kube-scheduler/framework/listers.go) to add `AssumedInThisCycleCount() int` to the `PodGroupState` interface.

### 2. Cache Implementation
Modify [pkg/scheduler/backend/cache/podgroupstate.go](file:///usr/local/google/home/vshkrabkov/Development/kubernetes/pkg/scheduler/backend/cache/podgroupstate.go):
*   Implement `AssumedInThisCycleCount() int` for `podGroupState` (live state) to return `0`.
*   Update `podGroupStateSnapshot` struct to track pods assumed during the cycle:
    ```go
    type podGroupStateSnapshot struct {
        podGroupStateData
        assumedThisCycle sets.Set[types.UID]
    }
    ```
*   Update `assumePod` method on `podGroupStateSnapshot` to add the pod UID to `assumedThisCycle`.
*   Update `forgetPod` method on `podGroupStateSnapshot` to remove the pod UID from `assumedThisCycle`.
*   Implement `AssumedInThisCycleCount() int` for `podGroupStateSnapshot` to return `len(s.assumedThisCycle)`.

### 3. Gang Scheduling Plugin
Modify [pkg/scheduler/framework/plugins/gangscheduling/gangscheduling.go](file:///usr/local/google/home/vshkrabkov/Development/kubernetes/pkg/scheduler/framework/plugins/gangscheduling/gangscheduling.go):
*   In `PlacementFeasible`, simplify `alreadyScheduledCount` calculation:
    ```go
    scheduled := podGroupState.ScheduledPodsCount()
    assumedInThisCycle := podGroupState.AssumedInThisCycleCount()
    alreadyScheduledCount := scheduled - assumedInThisCycle
    ```
*   Remove the `unscheduledUIDs` set construction and the loop over `podGroupState.ScheduledPods()`.

### 4. Test Mocks and Contracts
*   Update `podGroupStateContract` in [pkg/scheduler/framework/autoscaler_contract/lister_contract_test.go](file:///usr/local/google/home/vshkrabkov/Development/kubernetes/pkg/scheduler/framework/autoscaler_contract/lister_contract_test.go) to implement `AssumedInThisCycleCount() int` (returning `0`).
*   Update `mockPodGroupState` in [pkg/scheduler/framework/plugins/gangscheduling/gangscheduling_test.go](file:///usr/local/google/home/vshkrabkov/Development/kubernetes/pkg/scheduler/framework/plugins/gangscheduling/gangscheduling_test.go) to implement `AssumedInThisCycleCount() int`.
*   Add unit tests in [pkg/scheduler/backend/cache/podgroupstate_test.go](file:///usr/local/google/home/vshkrabkov/Development/kubernetes/pkg/scheduler/backend/cache/podgroupstate_test.go) to verify that `AssumedInThisCycleCount` correctly tracks assumed pods in a snapshot.

Links & References:
* PR Discussion: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/139464#discussion_r3569622119

title: Kubelet allocates system-reserved CPU to pod when cpu-topology is hybrid
author: ksadura
body:
### What happened?

Following CPU topology (hybrid i.e. cores with id `0-28` are hyper-threaded and `32-43` are single-threaded) was given.
```
Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-14700
CPU(s):                28
On-line CPU(s) list:   0-27
Core(s) per socket:    20
Socket(s):             1
NUMA node(s):          1
```

For pod with 2 guaranteed CPUs, kubelet allocated `0-1` cpuset, despite `0` was explicitly reserved for system purposes. Kubelet was launched with following flags: `--kubelet-flags="--cpu-manager-policy=static --reserved-cpus=0`. 

Here are some kubelet logs:
```
I0710 16:32:08.192397  353056 policy_static.go:185] "Reserved CPUs not available for exclusive assignment" reservedSize=1 reserved="0" reservedPhysicalCPUs="0-1"
...
I0710 16:32:45.032352  353056 policy_static.go:556] "Allocate start" pod="default/test-7fb87c954c-n8gtr" podUID="35863126-0027-47ff-90f9-8684692a19bb" containerName="testcnt1"
I0710 16:32:45.032419  353056 policy_static.go:675] "AllocateCPUs" pod="default/test-7fb87c954c-n8gtr" podUID="35863126-0027-47ff-90f9-8684692a19bb" containerName="testcnt1" numCPUs=2 socket=null
I0710 16:32:45.035206  353056 policy_static.go:708] "AllocateCPUs" pod="default/test-7fb87c954c-n8gtr" podUID="35863126-0027-47ff-90f9-8684692a19bb" containerName="testcnt1" result="0-1 aligned=<uncore:true>"
I0710 16:32:45.037033  353056 policy_static.go:621] "Allocate end" pod="default/test-7fb87c954c-n8gtr" podUID="35863126-0027-47ff-90f9-8684692a19bb" containerName="testcnt1"
```

Then, kubelet was **restarted**, but it can't boot again due to invalid state:
```
I0713 16:37:04.270175  593834 state_mem.go:143] "Updated default CPUSet" logger="CPUManager state checkpoint.CPUManager state memory" cpuSet="2-27"
I0713 16:37:04.270195  593834 state_mem.go:161] "Updated CPUSet assignments" logger="CPUManager state checkpoint.CPUManager state memory" assignments="{\"35863126-0027-47ff-90f9-8684692a19bb\":{\"testcnt1\":\"0-1\"}}"
I0713 15:37:04.270215  593834 state_checkpoint.go:182] "restored state from checkpoint" logger="CPUManager state checkpoint"
I0713 16:37:04.270227  593834 state_checkpoint.go:183] "defaultCPUSet" logger="CPUManager state checkpoint" defaultCpuSet="2-27"
E0713 16:37:04.270257  593834 policy_static.go:303] "invalid state, please drain node and remove policy state file" err="not all reserved cpus: \"0\" are present in defaultCpuSet: \"2-27\""
E0713 16:37:04.270266  593834 cpu_manager.go:263] "Policy start error" err="not all reserved cpus: \"0\" are present in defaultCpuSet: \"2-27\""
E0713 16:37:04.270277  593834 kubelet.go:1837] "Failed to start ContainerManager" err="start cpu manager error: not all reserved cpus: \"0\" are present in defaultCpuSet: \"2-27\""
```

### What did you expect to happen?

Kubelet allocates 2 CPUs that do not interfere with reserved CPUs. After restart kubelet starts smoothly.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Start local cluster using `hack/local-up-cluster.sh` script with proper flags set.
```console
CPUMANAGER_POLICY="static" KUBELET_FLAGS="--reserved-cpus=0" hack/local-up-cluster.sh
```
2. Deploy the pod with guaranteed CPU resources
```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: '2'
    memory: "2Gi"
  limits:
    cpu: '2'
    memory: "2Gi"
```
3. Kill the cluster and try to start it again.

### Anything else we need to know?

--

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.36.0
Kustomize Version: v5.8.1
Server Version: v1.37.0-alpha.2.283+fde2a0d4e7ab62-dirty
```

</details>


### Cloud provider

<details>
N/A
</details>


### OS version

<details>

```console
$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="24.04"
VERSION="24.04.4 LTS (Noble Numbat)"
VERSION_CODENAME=noble
ID=ubuntu
ID_LIKE=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"
UBUNTU_CODENAME=noble
LOGO=ubuntu-logo
$ uname -a
Linux 6.17.0-35-generic #35~24.04.1-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Tue May 26 19:30:42 UTC 2 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

```

</details>

title: Reinstate or drop `[Feature:PerformanceDNS]` tests
author: danwinship
body:
https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/pull/37410 points out that the `[Feature:PerformanceDNS]` tests are not currently being run anywhere.

We should either fix this so that there is some CI jobs that runs them, or else remove them if they are no longer useful.

/sig network
/priority backlog

title: Add `[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]` CI
author: danwinship
body:
https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/pull/37410 points out that we are not currently running `[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]` tests anywhere. We were previously running them in some kind jobs, but the default kernel used in GCE jobs now doesn't support SCTP (https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/35031).

As I understand it, the GCE underlay network itself also does not support SCTP, so we can't use normal-GCE-networking-on-Ubuntu-on-GCE either. (I might be wrong about that? If the GCE underlay does support SCTP, then the fix is to just remove `SCTPConnectivity` from the skips on `pull-kubernetes-e2e-gce-network-policies` / `ci-kubernetes-e2e-gce-network-policies`.)

Kind-on-Ubuntu-on-GCE should work (since it would only send SCTP over the docker network, not the GCE network), but I'm not sure if our scripts support that setup. (We have kind-on-GCE and Ubuntu-on-GCE but there are no kind-on-Ubuntu-on-GCE jobs.)

I believe the EKS/AKS kernel and underlays all support SCTP, so the final possibility is to run a (kind or non-kind) jobs outside of GCE. (In which case, this could possibly be combined with #140489.)

Note that some of the `[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]` tests are also `[Feature:NetworkPolicy]`, so we want to run these on a job that also includes NP support.

cc @aojea

title: Add `[Feature:Networking-IPv6]` and `[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]` CI
author: danwinship
body:
https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/pull/37410 points out that the single `[Feature:Networking-IPv6]` e2e test and the `[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]` tests do not get run by any of our existing CI jobs.

`[Feature:Networking-IPv6]` requires connectivity to the IPv6 internet, which our default CI environment doesn't have.
`[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]` requires kernel support for SCTP (which the default GCE "COS" kernels don't have) and pod network support for SCTP (which, AIUI, the GCE underlay network does not have, so these tests can't run in an environment that sends pod network packets directly over the GCE underlay). Also, some of the SCTP tests are also `[Feature:NetworkPolicy]`, so we should run it in an environment with NP. And one of the SCTP tests is `[Feature:IPv6DualStack]`.

@upodroid [points out](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/pull/37410#issuecomment-4941003690) that the `k8s-infra-aks-prow-build` cluster supports dual-stack and has external IPv6 connectivity. It seems that the `eks-prow-build-cluster` also supports dual-stack clusters, though I'm not sure if it supports dual-stack external connectivity.

I'm not sure about the state of SCTP support in the default kernels on those platforms, or if we have infra for running non-default-kernels on jobs on those platforms...

Anyway, we should add a periodic that runs on EKS or AKS with a config that allows for `[Feature:IPv6DualStack]`, `[Feature:Networking-IPv6]`, `[Feature:SCTPConnectivity]`, and `[Feature:NetworkPolicy]`.

/sig network
/sig testing
/area ipv6
/area network-policy

cc @aojea

title: PodRequests/PodLimits insert an explicit zero for pod-level resource keys absent from actuated resources
author: semx
body:
**What happened**:

`PodRequests` and `PodLimits` (`staging/src/k8s.io/component-helpers/resource/helpers.go`) compute pod-level aggregation by iterating `pod.Spec.Resources.{Requests,Limits}` and assigning the value from the *effective* (actuated) resources for each supported pod-level resource:

```go
for resourceName, quantity := range pod.Spec.Resources.Limits {
    if IsSupportedPodLevelResource(resourceName) {
        limits[resourceName] = quantity
        if effectiveLims != nil {
            limits[resourceName] = effectiveLims[resourceName]
        }
    }
}
```

When a resource key is present in the spec but absent from the effective map, `effectiveLims[resourceName]` returns a zero-valued `resource.Quantity`, so the key is inserted into the result with an explicit zero.

This happens in practice when an in-place resize *adds* a pod-level request/limit (adding a key is permitted by validation; only removal is blocked) and the resize is marked `PodResizePending` / `Infeasible`: `determineEffective{Requests,Limits}` then returns the actuated-only map, which does not contain the newly added key.

Container-level aggregation (`AggregateContainerRequests` / `AggregateContainerLimits`) iterates the *effective* map via `addResourceList` and therefore omits keys that are not actuated. The two paths disagree on the resulting key set for the same input.

**What you expected to happen**:

Pod-level aggregation should omit a key that is not present in the effective/actuated resources, matching container-level aggregation, rather than inserting an explicit zero.

**How to reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)**:

Build a pod with pod-level resources (`Spec.Resources.{Requests,Limits}` = `{cpu, memory}`), status resources actuated to `{cpu}` only, and a `PodResizePending` / `Infeasible` condition; call `PodLimits` / `PodRequests` with `UseStatusResources` and `InPlacePodLevelResourcesVerticalScalingEnabled` set. The returned map contains `memory: 0` (explicit), whereas `AggregateContainer{Requests,Limits}` on the same pod omits `memory`.

**Anything else we need to know?**:

This is a consistency issue and is not currently observable through any cgroup/enforcement path. The paths that write cgroup values (`ResourceConfigForPod`, `calculateSandboxResources`) do not set `UseStatusResources`, and the consumers that do set it read through `.Cpu()` / `.Memory()` accessors, where an explicit zero and an absent key are equivalent. The only presence-checking consumer (`ResourceConfigForPod`, which checks `limits[v1.ResourceMemory]`) does not use those options. Filing ahead of the pod-level in-place resize GA work so the two aggregation paths stay consistent.

/sig node
/kind bug

title: resource: PodRequests/PodLimits report a zero-valued pod-level resource on an infeasible resize
author: semx

title: DRA: ResourceSlice validRange.step of 2^64 re-panics with integer divide by zero (bypasses the #139698 Sign() guard via Value() truncation)
author: thc1006
body:
### What happened?

`ResourceSlice` validation panics with `integer divide by zero` on a `capacity.requestPolicy.validRange.step` whose value is a positive multiple of 2^64 (for example `18446744073709551616`).

This re-opens the panic that #139653 reported and #139698 fixed. That fix added a guard using `Quantity.Sign()`, but the arithmetic still uses `Quantity.Value()`, and the two disagree for values that do not fit in int64:

```go
// pkg/apis/resource/validation/validation.go
if valueRange.Step.Sign() <= 0 {            // :1178  Sign() is exact -> 2^64 has Sign()==1, passes
    ...
} else {
    ... validateRequestPolicyRangeStep(...) // :1186
}

func validateRequestPolicyRangeStep(value, min, step apiresource.Quantity, ...) {
    stepVal := step.Value()   // :1197  Value() == big.Int.Int64(), truncates 2^64 -> 0
    ...
    if added%stepVal != 0 {   // :1201  added % 0 -> integer divide by zero
```

`resource.MustParse("18446744073709551616").Sign()` is `1` but `.Value()` is `0`, so a step of 2^64 passes the `Sign() <= 0` guard and then divides by zero. Same for `2^65`, `2^128`, and milli-scaled values like `18446744073709551616000m`.

### What did you expect to happen?

Validation should return a field error for an out-of-range step, never panic.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create (or have a DRA driver publish) a `ResourceSlice` whose device has `allowMultipleAllocations: true` and a capacity with:

```yaml
requestPolicy:
  default: "0"
  validRange:
    min: "0"
    step: "18446744073709551616"   # 2^64
```

Driving the real `validateRequestPolicyRange` with `step = 2^64` panics:

```
PANIC: runtime error: integer divide by zero
```

I have the test and can open a PR.

### Anything else we need to know?

The apiserver's panic-recovery middleware catches this and returns HTTP 500 for the offending create/update, so the apiserver does not crash; the effect is repeated 500s and log spam on the affected request. `CapacityRequestPolicy` lives only on `ResourceSlice.Spec`, which NodeRestriction limits to a node writing its own slice, so the realistic trigger is a buggy or malicious DRA driver publishing a slice (or a cluster-admin), not an unprivileged namespace tenant. It is gated by `DRAConsumableCapacity`, which is beta and on by default in 1.36, so the default configuration is affected.

There is a sibling wrong-accept in the same function: `added := value.Value() - min.Value()` (`:1200`) is an int64 subtraction that can wrap for near-`MaxInt64` `min`/`value`, producing an incorrect "not a multiple" result. Same root cause (int64-truncating `Value()` arithmetic).

Suggested fix: do the step/multiple math with `Quantity.Cmp` / `big.Int` (like the `Sign()` guard) instead of `Value()`, or reject a step whose `Value() <= 0` in addition to `Sign() <= 0`. Related `Value()`-truncation validation issue in the same file: #140457.

/sig node
/area dra

title: DRA: re-preparing a claim after a kubelet restart appends duplicate devices (unbounded checkpoint growth)
author: thc1006
body:
### What happened?

When the kubelet restarts while a pod using a ResourceClaim is still running, the DRA manager re-runs `NodePrepareResources` for that claim and appends the returned devices onto the checkpoint-restored `DriverState.Devices` instead of replacing them. The device list grows by one full set on every restart, and duplicate `CDIDeviceIDs` are surfaced to the runtime and persisted to the checkpoint.

`newClaimInfoFromState` restores every checkpointed claim with `prepared: false`:

```go
// pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/claiminfo.go
func newClaimInfoFromState(state *state.ClaimInfoState) *ClaimInfo {
	info := &ClaimInfo{
		ClaimInfoState: *state.DeepCopy(),
		prepared:       false,
	}
	return info
}
```

`prepared` is unexported and not part of the checkpoint, so it is always `false` after a restart. In `prepareResources` the skip guard then does not fire:

```go
// pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/manager.go
if claimInfo.isPrepared() { // false after a restart, so not skipped
	continue
}
```

`NodePrepareResources` runs again and the response is added with `addDevice`, which appends without checking for devices already present:

```go
// pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/claiminfo.go
func (info *ClaimInfo) addDevice(driverName string, deviceState state.Device) {
	...
	driverState.Devices = append(driverState.Devices, deviceState)
	info.DriverState[driverName] = driverState
}
```

The cached ClaimInfo it appends to is the one restored from the checkpoint (`prepareResources` uses `m.cache.get` and only builds a fresh ClaimInfo when the entry does not exist), so the list doubles. `setPrepared` then checkpoints the doubled list, and the next restart doubles it again.

### What did you expect to happen?

Re-preparing an already-prepared claim after a restart should be idempotent: the manager should not accumulate duplicate devices, and the checkpoint should not grow across restarts.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

A unit test in `pkg/kubelet/cm/dra` that drives the real `Manager`: prepare a claim, then build a second `Manager` on the same state directory (which restores the checkpoint), then prepare the same pod again. `DriverState.Devices` goes from 1 to 2. Making `addDevice` idempotent (or resetting the driver's devices before re-adding) keeps it at 1.

```
after first prepare: 1 device(s) in DriverState
after restart + re-prepare: 2 device(s) in DriverState
```

I have the test and am glad to attach it or open a PR.

### Anything else we need to know?

Not tenant-triggered and node-local. The concrete effect is unbounded growth of `DriverState.Devices` and the checkpoint for a long-lived pod on a kubelet that restarts (upgrades, crashes, config reloads), plus duplicate `CDIDeviceIDs` passed to the runtime and to device-health tracking.

Suggested direction: make re-prepare idempotent, either by resetting the driver's `DriverState.Devices` before re-adding the `NodePrepareResources` response (the response is the authoritative set) or by deduping in `addDevice`. Restoring with `prepared: false` and re-calling the driver is fine; the manager just should not accumulate.

/sig node
/area dra

title: DRA: reject negative ConsumedCapacity before computing node-allocatable resource demand
author: thc1006

title: Watch client shutdown: three remaining bugs from #131505
author: karlkfi
body:
In 2025 I fixed several watch client shutdown bugs: #131266 (reflector Stop deadlock), #131321 (VeryShortWatchError), #131706 (watch test body cleanup). Thanks again to @wojtek-t and @liggitt for those reviews.

The test work in #131505 turned up three more bugs. They were documented there as TODOs but never fixed. I've verified all three still exist on master (843726939e9) and plan to send one small PR per bug:

1. **Response body leak.** `Request.Watch` doesn't close `resp.Body` when content-type negotiation fails. Tracked in #140464.

2. **Error event after Stop.** `StreamWatcher.receive` selects between the done channel and the error send. When both are ready Go picks at random, so a decode error racing with `Stop()` reaches the consumer about half the time. First reported back in 2015 (#6433) and again recently in #139782. The open fix #139783 adds the http2 error string to `IsProbableEOF`, which helps that case, but the race allows any decode error through after Stop. My PR will check the done channel first so a stopped watcher just closes its result channel.

3. **Broken error unwrapping.** `StreamWatcher.receive` and the client watch decoder wrap decode errors with `%v`, which severs `errors.Is`/`errors.As`. Consumers end up string-matching the message. Config Sync, for example, matches the literal "unable to decode an event from the watch stream: context canceled" text to detect shutdown. Small enough to fix without its own issue.

Each PR is small, structured as a failing test commit followed by the fix, with race detector and stress output in the description.

Requesting `/triage accepted` so the fix PRs land against accepted issues.

/kind bug
/sig api-machinery

title: client-go: Request.Watch leaks the response body when content-type negotiation fails
author: karlkfi
body:
### What happened?

When `Request.Watch` gets a 200 response but content-type negotiation fails (no stream decoder for the response content type), `newStreamWatcher` returns the error without closing `resp.Body`. The connection leaks.

This is in `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/request.go`. It was found by the test work in #131505 and left there as a TODO. I've verified it's still present on master (843726939e9).

### What did you expect to happen?

The response body is closed on every error path out of `Request.Watch`, the same way the retry paths already handle it via `readAndCloseResponseBody`.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Extend the existing `TestWatchUnknownContentType` in `rest/request_test.go` to count body closes with the in-package `readTracker` helper:

```
=== RUN   TestWatchUnknownContentType
    request_test.go:2271: Expected response body Close to be invoked 1 time, but got: 0
--- FAIL: TestWatchUnknownContentType (0.00s)
```

### Anything else we need to know?

I have a fix ready, roughly 4 lines plus the test above. It reuses the existing `readAndCloseResponseBody` helper so the connection can be reused. PR incoming shortly.

/kind bug
/sig api-machinery

### Kubernetes version

<details>

master (843726939e9). Client library bug, applies to client-go at HEAD.

</details>
